title: The Tableau Algorithm for Modal Logic

charts.title: Topic Map
charts.description: Overview of propositional and modal tableau algorithms, their rules, and how to use them to decide satisfiability and validity in modal logic.

nodes.label:
Week 10
Tableau Algorithm
nodes.note: Week 10 introduces the tableau algorithm: a systematic decision procedure for satisfiability and validity in propositional and modal logic, overcoming the issue that Kripke models can be infinite.

nodes.label:
Propositional
Tableau
nodes.note: A two-column True/False table whose rules decompose Boolean formulas. Closed branches mean contradiction; an open fully-expanded branch yields a satisfying valuation.

nodes.label:
Modal
Tableau
nodes.note: An extension of the propositional tableau with tagged formulas (w : F) and a third Relation column tracking the accessibility relation between worlds.

nodes.label:
Tagged
Formulas
nodes.note: A tagged formula (w : F) records that formula F is being asserted at world w. This is how the tableau accounts for world-relative truth in modal logic.

nodes.label:
Tableau
Rules
nodes.note: Eight propositional rules handle the Boolean connectives in True and False, while four new modal rules handle box and diamond.

nodes.label:
Modal Rules
(Diamond, Box)
nodes.note: Diamond-true and Box-false introduce fresh world labels. Box-true and Diamond-false propagate formulas across existing relations and must NOT be marked as expanded.

nodes.label: Satisfiability
nodes.note: Initial tableau places (w0 : F) in True. Expand using the rules; if any branch is fully expanded and open, F is satisfiable and a model can be read off.

nodes.label: Validity
nodes.note: F is valid iff not F is unsatisfiable. Place (w0 : F) in False and expand; F is valid iff every branch closes.

nodes.label:
Read-off
Kripke Model
nodes.note: From an open branch: worlds are the labels appearing in tagged formulas, the relation is the Relation column, and the valuation is given by True/False of atomic tagged formulas.

nodes.label:
Frame Class
Restrictions
nodes.note: To check satisfiability in a frame class such as reflexive or transitive, close the Relation column under the required property after each rule that adds new pairs.

nodes.label:
Looping &
Blocking
nodes.note: Some satisfiable formulas on transitive frames cause the tableau to spawn infinitely many fresh worlds. Loop-checking reuses existing world labels to ensure termination.

nodes.label:
Tree Model
Property
nodes.note: The tableau procedure always produces models with tree-shaped frames. Hence every satisfiable modal formula is satisfiable in a tree model.

charts.title: Propositional Tableau Algorithm
charts.description: Step-by-step procedure for deciding satisfiability of a propositional formula using the tableau method.

nodes.label: Formula F
nodes.note: We begin with a propositional formula F and want to determine whether it is satisfiable using the tableau decision procedure.

nodes.label:
Place F in
True column
nodes.note: Create the initial tableau with F in the True column and an empty False column. This represents the assumption that F should be true.

nodes.label:
Unexpanded formula
remains?
nodes.note: Check whether any compound formula in either column has not yet been processed by the corresponding expansion rule.

nodes.label:
Select unexpanded
formula
nodes.note: Choose any unexpanded formula. The order does not affect the final answer, only the size of the resulting tree.

nodes.label: Apply expansion rule
nodes.note: Apply the appropriate expansion rule. Branching rules (or-true, and-false, imp-true) split the tableau into two sub-tableaux.

nodes.label: Any branch open?
nodes.note: After full expansion, check whether at least one branch is open: that is, the True and False columns share no common formula.

nodes.label: SATISFIABLE
nodes.note: An open branch exists. F is satisfiable; the satisfying assignment sets V(p) = 1 for each propositional variable in the True column of that branch.

nodes.label: UNSATISFIABLE
nodes.note: Every branch closed (some formula appears in both True and False). F is unsatisfiable - no valuation can make it true.

charts.title: Modal Tableau Algorithm
charts.description: Decision procedure for modal satisfiability using tagged formulas and the Relation column.

nodes.label: Modal formula F
nodes.note: We want to decide whether F is satisfiable in some Kripke model. We use the modal tableau extension of the propositional procedure.

nodes.label:
Place (w0 : F)
in True column
nodes.note: Initialise the tableau with the tagged formula (w0 : F) in the True column. The Relation column is empty.

nodes.label:
Any rule
applicable?
nodes.note: Check whether any rule (propositional or modal) can still add new tagged formulas or relations to the current tableau.

nodes.label:
Propositional or
modal rule?
nodes.note: Choose between a propositional rule that decomposes Boolean structure and a modal rule that introduces or propagates information across worlds.

nodes.label:
Apply propositional
rule on tag
nodes.note: Apply the propositional True/False rule to the tagged formula. The world label stays the same; only the Boolean structure is decomposed.

nodes.label:
Diamond-true /
Box-false:
add fresh world
nodes.note: These rules introduce a brand-new world label u not appearing elsewhere in the branch, plus the corresponding (w, u) entry in Relation.

nodes.label:
Box-true /
Diamond-false:
propagate to all u
nodes.note: These rules add tagged formulas (u : F) for every existing relation (w, u) in Relation. Do NOT mark them as expanded; reapply when new relations appear.

nodes.label:
Any branch fully
expanded and open?
nodes.note: After all rules have been applied as far as possible, check whether at least one branch contains no contradictory tagged formula.

nodes.label:
SATISFIABLE
(read off model)
nodes.note: An open branch exists, so F is satisfiable. Build a Kripke model from its world labels, the Relation column, and the True/False values of atomic tagged formulas.

nodes.label: UNSATISFIABLE
nodes.note: Every branch closed, so F is unsatisfiable in any Kripke model. There is no Kripke world at which F can be true.

charts.title: Tableau Rules Taxonomy
charts.description: Classification of all the propositional and modal tableau rules into True column, False column, and modal categories.

nodes.label: Tableau Rules
nodes.note: There are eight propositional rules (one True and one False per connective) plus four modal rules. Each rule has a precise application and may or may not branch.

nodes.label:
True Column
Rules
nodes.note: Rules that fire when a compound formula appears in the True column. They decompose its top-level connective into smaller assertions.

nodes.label:
False Column
Rules
nodes.note: Rules that fire when a compound formula appears in the False column. They decompose into smaller assertions on the appropriate side.

nodes.label: Modal Rules
nodes.note: Four extra rules for the modal operators box and diamond. Two introduce fresh worlds; two propagate across existing relations.

nodes.label: not-true
nodes.note: If not-F is in True then F goes to False. Non-branching.

nodes.label: and-true
nodes.note: If F-and-G is in True then both F and G go to True. Non-branching.

nodes.label: or-true
nodes.note: If F-or-G is in True, branch into two sub-tableaux: one with F in True and one with G in True.

nodes.label: imp-true
nodes.note: If F-imp-G is in True, branch into F in False or G in True (since F-imp-G is equivalent to not-F or G).

nodes.label: not-false
nodes.note: If not-F is in False then F goes to True. Non-branching.

nodes.label: and-false
nodes.note: If F-and-G is in False, branch into F in False or G in False.

nodes.label: or-false
nodes.note: If F-or-G is in False then both F and G go to False. Non-branching.

nodes.label: imp-false
nodes.note: If F-imp-G is in False then F goes to True and G goes to False. Non-branching.

nodes.label:
diamond-true
(fresh u)
nodes.note: If (w : diamond F) is in True, introduce a fresh world label u, add (u : F) to True and (w, u) to Relation.

nodes.label:
box-false
(fresh u)
nodes.note: If (w : box F) is in False, introduce a fresh world label u, add (u : F) to False and (w, u) to Relation.

nodes.label:
box-true
(propagate)
nodes.note: If (w : box F) is in True, add (u : F) to True for every (w, u) in Relation. Do NOT mark this rule as expanded.

nodes.label:
diamond-false
(propagate)
nodes.note: If (w : diamond F) is in False, add (u : F) to False for every (w, u) in Relation. Do NOT mark this rule as expanded.

charts.title: Reading off a Kripke Model from an Open Tableau
charts.description: How to extract a satisfying Kripke model from a fully-expanded open branch of a modal tableau.

nodes.label:
Fully expanded
open branch
nodes.note: Start from any branch of the modal tableau that has been fully expanded and contains no contradictory tagged formula.

nodes.label:
Collect world labels
appearing in tags
nodes.note: List every distinct world label w that appears in any tagged formula on the branch. These become the worlds W of the Kripke model.

nodes.label:
Set R = Relation
column
nodes.note: Take the accessibility relation R of the model to be exactly the set of pairs (w, u) listed in the Relation column of the branch.

nodes.label:
Set V(w,p) = 1
iff (w : p) in True
nodes.note: Define the valuation: a propositional variable p holds at world w iff the tagged atom (w : p) appears in the True column of the branch.

nodes.label:
Other atoms
default to false
nodes.note: Atoms not mentioned anywhere on the branch can be assigned any value; conventionally we set them to false to keep the model minimal.

nodes.label:
Kripke model
(W, R, V) satisfies F
nodes.note: The constructed Kripke model is guaranteed to satisfy the original formula F at world w0, by soundness of the tableau procedure.
